title: Disqualification of candidates following a criminal conviction
p1:
A criminal conviction can do more than affect a person's reputation.

It can also decide whether someone is legally allowed to contest an election. But many people are surprised to learn that a conviction does not always lead to immediate disqualification.
p2:
A common misconception is that every criminal case makes a candidate ineligible. The reality is different. The law usually looks at factors such as the nature of the offence, the punishment imposed, and whether the conviction meets specific legal conditions.

A person facing only an allegation is treated differently from someone who has been convicted by a court. This distinction protects the principle that a person is considered innocent until proven guilty.
p3:
The key takeaway is that eligibility to contest elections is not decided by public opinion alone. It depends on the legal consequences attached to a conviction.

This balance tries to protect both electoral integrity and individual rights. A conviction can carry serious consequences, but those consequences follow a legal process, not just an accusation.
conclusion: Should candidates facing criminal convictions be disqualified immediately, or should the law wait until the final stages of the judicial process?
